revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

revision.comment: とりあえず。内湯と項目混同が若干あるため、今後要修正
revision.text:
'''外湯'''（そとゆ）とは、[[温泉街]]に存在する、宿泊施設を伴わない[[公衆浴場]]、日帰り入浴施設のこと。 [[内湯]]に対する[[対義語]]である。
源泉開発の技術が乏しかった時代、温泉は自然湧出源泉に限られていた。湯船は源泉が湧出する場所に共同の浴場として整備され、[[旅館]]はその周辺に建てられた。宿泊客は温泉に浸かる際には、旅館の外にある共同浴場に通っていた。[[大正時代]]以降、源泉開発技術が向上するにつれ、旅館ごとに独自の源泉を持つようになった。その際に、旅館独自の湯と旅館の外の湯ということで、内湯、外湯という区分が生じた。古くからの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]は、皆外湯に通う形態であったが、今日では[[俵山温泉]]など一部に残るのみである。
現在の外湯の多くは、地元の人が管理している共同浴場である。
また、源泉の数、湧出量が豊富な温泉地、温泉街では、温泉を利用した浴場が多数存在する。異なる源泉、異なる浴場を巡ることが温泉地の楽しみの一つとなっている。以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。 *[[湯田中温泉]] *[[渋温泉]] *[[城崎温泉]]
[[Category:温泉|*そとゆ]]

'''外湯'''（そとゆ）とは、[[温泉街]]に存在する、宿泊施設を伴わない[[公衆浴場]]、日帰り入浴施設のこと。[[内湯]]に対する[[対義語]]である。
源泉開発の技術が乏しかった時代、[[温泉]]は自然湧出源泉に限られていた。湯量が限られていたことや、温泉は皆の共有財産で誰かの占有物ではない、という考えから湯船は源泉が湧出する場所に共同の浴場として整備され、[[旅館]]はその周辺に建てられた。宿泊客は温泉に浸かる際には、旅館の外にある共同浴場に通っていた。[[大正時代]]以降、源泉開発技術が向上するにつれ、旅館ごとに独自の源泉を持つようになった。その際に、旅館独自の湯と旅館の外の湯ということで、内湯、外湯という区分が生じた。
古くからの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]は、皆外湯に通う形態であったが、今日では[[俵山温泉]]、[[温湯温泉 (青森県)|温湯温泉]]など一部に残るのみである。
外湯の多くは、地元の人が管理している共同浴場であったが、今日では日帰り入浴施設も多く存在する。
== 外湯巡り == 源泉の数、湧出量が豊富な温泉地、温泉街では、温泉を利用した浴場が多数存在する。異なる源泉、異なる浴場を巡ることが温泉地の楽しみの一つとなっている。以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。
* [[飯坂温泉]] * [[湯田中温泉]] * [[渋温泉]] * [[城崎温泉]]
[[Category:温泉|*そとゆ]]

'''外湯'''（'''そとゆ'''）とは、[[温泉街]]に存在する、宿泊施設を伴わない[[公衆浴場]]、日帰り入浴施設のこと。[[内湯]]に対する[[対義語]]である。
[[画像:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|thumb|220px|[[城崎温泉]]「さとの湯」]] 源泉開発の技術が乏しかった時代、[[温泉]]は自然湧出源泉に限られていた。湯量が限られていたことや、温泉は皆の共有財産で誰かの占有物ではない、という考えから湯船は源泉が湧出する場所に共同の浴場として整備され、[[旅館]]はその周辺に建てられた。宿泊客は温泉に浸かる際には、旅館の外にある[[共同浴場]]に通っていた。[[大正時代]]以降、源泉開発技術が向上するにつれ、旅館ごとに独自の源泉を持つようになった。その際に、旅館独自の湯と旅館の外の湯ということで、内湯、外湯という区分が生じた。源泉の意から、元湯と呼ぶ温泉もある。
古くからの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]は、皆外湯に通う形態であったが、今日では[[俵山温泉]]、[[温湯温泉 (青森県)|温湯温泉]]など一部に残るのみである。
外湯の多くは、地元の人が管理している共同浴場であったが、今日では休息施設を併設した日帰り入浴施設も多く存在する。
== 外湯巡り == 源泉の数、湧出量が豊富な温泉地、温泉街では、温泉を利用した浴場が多数存在する。異なる源泉、異なる浴場を巡ることが温泉地の楽しみの一つとなっている。以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。

[[画像:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|thumb|220px|[[城崎温泉]]「さとの湯」]] 源泉開発の技術が乏しかった時代、[[温泉]]は自然湧出源泉に限られていた。湯量が限られていたことや、温泉は皆の共有財産で誰かの占有物ではない、という考えから湯船は源泉が湧出する場所に共同の浴場として整備され、[[旅館]]はその周辺に建てられた。宿泊客は温泉に浸かる際には、旅館の外にある[[共同浴場]]に通っていた。[[大正時代]]以降、源泉開発技術が向上するにつれ、旅館ごとに独自の源泉を持つようになった。その際に、旅館独自の湯と旅館の外の湯ということで、内湯、外湯という区分が生じた。源泉の意から、元湯と呼ぶ温泉もある。
古くからの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]は、皆外湯に通う形態であったが、今日では[[俵山温泉]]、[[温湯温泉 (青森県)|温湯温泉]]など一部に残るのみである。
外湯の多くは、地元の人が管理している共同浴場であったが、今日では休息施設を併設した日帰り入浴施設も多く存在する。
== 外湯巡り == 源泉の数、湧出量が豊富な温泉地、温泉街では、温泉を利用した浴場が多数存在する。異なる源泉、異なる浴場を巡ることが温泉地の楽しみの一つとなっている。以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。
* [[飯坂温泉]] * [[湯田中温泉]] * [[渋温泉]] * [[野沢温泉]] * [[城崎温泉]] * [[別府温泉]]：別府市内には地元の人が管理する共同浴場が数百ヶ所ある。 **[[別府八湯温泉道]]：それらの共同浴場や旅館の立ち寄り湯などを88湯巡り温泉名人の認定を目指すもの。
== ギャラリー == <gallery> 画像:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[有馬温泉]] [[金の湯]] 画像:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|[[野沢温泉]] 大湯 画像:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|[[三朝温泉]] たまわりの湯 画像:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[玉造温泉]]ゆ～ゆ </gallery>
== 関連項目 == * [[共同浴場]] - 温泉水を使用、地元民が管理する公衆浴場 * [[日帰り入浴施設]] - 温泉水を使用、企業等資本により建設された公衆浴場 * [[秘湯]] * [[野湯]] * [[仙人温泉]]
{{温泉}}

[[Category:温泉|そとゆ]]

'''外湯'''（そとゆ）とは、[[温泉街]]に存在する、宿泊施設を伴わない[[公衆浴場]]、日帰り入浴施設のこと。[[内湯]]に対する[[対義語]]である。
[[File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|thumb|220px|[[城崎温泉]]「さとの湯」]] 源泉開発の技術が乏しかった時代、[[温泉]]は自然湧出源泉に限られていた。湯量が限られていたことや、温泉は皆の共有財産で誰かの占有物ではない、という考えから湯船は源泉が湧出する場所に共同の浴場として整備され、[[旅館]]はその周辺に建てられた。宿泊客は温泉に浸かる際には、旅館の外にある[[共同浴場]]に通っていた。[[大正時代]]以降、源泉開発技術が向上するにつれ、旅館ごとに独自の源泉を持つようになった。その際に、旅館独自の湯と旅館の外の湯ということで、内湯、外湯という区分が生じた。源泉の意から、元湯と呼ぶ温泉もある。
古くからの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]は、皆外湯に通う形態であったが、今日では[[俵山温泉]]、[[温湯温泉 (青森県)|温湯温泉]]など一部に残るのみである。
外湯の多くは、地元の人が管理している共同浴場であったが、今日では休息施設を併設した日帰り入浴施設も多く存在する。
== 外湯巡り == 源泉の数、湧出量が豊富な温泉地、温泉街では、温泉を利用した浴場が多数存在する。異なる源泉、異なる浴場を巡ることが温泉地の楽しみの一つとなっている。以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。

== 概要 == [[源泉]]開発の技術が乏しかった時代、[[温泉]]は自然湧出源泉に限られていた。湯量が限られていたことや、温泉は皆の共有財産で誰かの占有物ではない、という考えから湯船は源泉が湧出する場所に共同の浴場として整備され、[[旅館]]はその周辺に建てられた。宿泊客は温泉に浸かる際には、旅館の外にある[[共同浴場]]に通っていた。[[大正時代]]以降、源泉開発技術が向上するにつれ、旅館ごとに独自の源泉を持つようになった。その際に、旅館独自の湯と旅館の外の湯ということで、内湯、外湯という区分が生じた。源泉の意から、元湯と呼ぶ温泉もある。
古くからの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]は、皆外湯に通う形態であったが、今日では[[俵山温泉]]、[[温湯温泉 (青森県)|温湯温泉]]、[[城崎温泉]]、など一部に残るのみである。
外湯の多くは、地元の人が管理している共同浴場であったが、今日では休息施設を併設した日帰り入浴施設も多く存在する。
== 外湯巡り == 源泉の数、湧出量が豊富な温泉地、温泉街では、温泉を利用した浴場が多数存在する。異なる源泉、異なる浴場を巡ることが温泉地の楽しみの一つとなっている。以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。
* [[上山温泉]] * [[飯坂温泉]] * [[湯田中温泉]] * [[渋温泉]] * [[野沢温泉]] * [[越後湯沢温泉]] * [[伊東温泉]] * [[城崎温泉]] * [[南紀白浜温泉]] * [[別府温泉]]：[[竹瓦温泉]]、[[鉄輪むし湯]]などの市営温泉の他、別府市内には地元の人が管理する[[共同温泉]]が数百ヶ所ある。 ** [[別府八湯温泉道]]：これらの共同温泉や旅館の立ち寄り湯などを88ヶ湯巡り温泉名人の認定を目指すもの。
== ギャラリー == <gallery> ファイル:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[有馬温泉]] [[金の湯]] ファイル:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|[[城崎温泉]]「さとの湯」 ファイル:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|[[野沢温泉]] 大湯 ファイル:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|[[三朝温泉]] たまわりの湯 ファイル:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[玉造温泉]] ゆ～ゆ ファイル:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[南紀白浜温泉]]「崎の湯」 ファイル:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[別府温泉]] [[竹瓦温泉]] ファイル:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|[[鉄輪温泉]] [[鉄輪むし湯]] </gallery>
== 関連項目 == * [[共同浴場]] - 温泉水を使用、地元民が管理する公衆浴場 * [[日帰り入浴施設]] - 温泉水を使用、企業等資本により建設された公衆浴場 * [[秘湯]] * [[野湯]] * [[仙人温泉]]

== 概要 == [[源泉]]開発の技術が乏しかった時代、[[温泉]]は自然湧出源泉に限られていた。湯量が限られていたことや、温泉は皆の共有財産で誰かの占有物ではない、という考えから湯船は源泉が湧出する場所に共同の浴場として整備され、[[旅館]]はその周辺に建てられた。宿泊客は温泉に浸かる際には、旅館の外にある[[共同浴場]]に通っていた。[[明治時代]]、[[大分県]]の[[別府温泉]]では[[源泉]]開発に[[上総掘り]]の技術が応用された湯突きが盛んになり、各旅館が独自の源泉を持つようになる。このような源泉開発はしだいに全国各地に普及し、その際に、旅館独自の湯と旅館の外の湯ということで、内湯、外湯という区分が生じた。源泉の意から、元湯と呼ぶ温泉もある。
古くからの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]は、皆外湯に通う形態であったが、今日では[[俵山温泉]]、[[温湯温泉 (青森県)|温湯温泉]]、[[城崎温泉]]、など一部に残るのみである。
外湯の多くは、地元の人が管理している共同浴場であったが、今日では休息施設を併設した日帰り入浴施設も多く存在する。
== 外湯巡り == 源泉の数、湧出量が豊富な温泉地、温泉街では、温泉を利用した浴場が多数存在する。異なる源泉、異なる浴場を巡ることが温泉地の楽しみの一つとなっている。以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。
* [[上山温泉]] * [[飯坂温泉]] * [[湯田中温泉]] * [[渋温泉]] * [[野沢温泉]] * [[越後湯沢温泉]] * [[伊東温泉]] * [[城崎温泉]] * [[南紀白浜温泉]] * [[別府温泉]]：[[竹瓦温泉]]、[[鉄輪むし湯]]などの市営温泉の他、別府市内には地元の人が管理する[[共同温泉]]が数百ヶ所ある。 ** [[別府八湯温泉道]]：これらの共同温泉や旅館の立ち寄り湯などを88ヶ湯巡り、温泉道名人の認定を目指すもの。
== ギャラリー == <gallery> ファイル:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[有馬温泉]] [[金の湯]] ファイル:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|[[城崎温泉]]「さとの湯」 ファイル:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|[[野沢温泉]] 大湯 ファイル:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|[[三朝温泉]] たまわりの湯 ファイル:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[玉造温泉]] ゆ～ゆ ファイル:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[南紀白浜温泉]]「崎の湯」 ファイル:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[別府温泉]] [[竹瓦温泉]] ファイル:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|[[鉄輪温泉]] [[鉄輪むし湯]] </gallery>
== 関連項目 == * [[共同浴場]] - 温泉水を使用、地元民が管理する公衆浴場。 * [[日帰り入浴施設]] - 主に温泉水を使用し（使用していない場合もある）、企業等資本により建設された公衆浴場。 * [[秘湯]] * [[野湯]] * [[仙人温泉]]
{{温泉}}
[[Category:温泉|そとゆ]]

{{出典の明記|date=2023年6月}} [[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[道後温泉]]本館（[[重要文化財]]）]] '''外湯'''（そとゆ）とは、[[温泉街]]に存在する、[[宿泊施設]]を伴わない[[公衆浴場]]、[[日帰り入浴施設]]のこと。[[内湯]]に対する[[対義語]]である。
== 概要 == [[源泉]]開発の技術が乏しかった時代、[[温泉]]は自然湧出源泉に限られていた。湯量が限られていたことや、温泉は皆の共有財産で誰かの占有物ではない、という考えから湯船は源泉が湧出する場所に共同の浴場として整備され、[[旅館]]はその周辺に建てられた。宿泊客は温泉に浸かる際には、旅館の外にある[[共同浴場]]に通っていた。[[明治時代]]、[[大分県]]の[[別府温泉]]では[[源泉]]開発に[[上総掘り]]の技術が応用された湯突きが盛んになり、各旅館が独自の源泉を持つようになる。このような源泉開発はしだいに全国各地に普及し、その際に、旅館独自の湯と旅館の外の湯ということで、内湯、外湯という区分が生じた。源泉の意から、元湯と呼ぶ温泉もある。
古くからの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]は、皆外湯に通う形態であったが、今日では[[俵山温泉]]、[[温湯温泉 (青森県)|温湯温泉]]、[[城崎温泉]]、など一部に残るのみである。
外湯の多くは、地元の人が管理している共同浴場であったが、今日では休息施設を併設した日帰り入浴施設も多く存在する。
== 外湯巡り == 源泉の数、湧出量が豊富な温泉地、温泉街では、温泉を利用した浴場が多数存在する。異なる源泉、異なる浴場を巡ることが温泉地の楽しみの一つとなっている。以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。
* [[上山温泉]] * [[飯坂温泉]] * [[湯田中温泉]] * [[渋温泉]] * [[野沢温泉]] * [[越後湯沢温泉]] * [[伊東温泉]] * [[城崎温泉]] * [[南紀白浜温泉]] * [[別府温泉]]：[[竹瓦温泉]]、[[鉄輪むし湯]]などの市営温泉の他、別府市内には地元の人が管理する[[共同温泉]]が数百か所ある。 ** [[別府八湯温泉道]]：これらの共同温泉や旅館の立ち寄り湯など88の湯を巡り、温泉道名人の認定を目指すもの。
== ギャラリー == <gallery> ファイル:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[有馬温泉]] [[金の湯]] ファイル:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|[[城崎温泉]]「さとの湯」 ファイル:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|[[野沢温泉]] 大湯 ファイル:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|[[三朝温泉]] たまわりの湯 ファイル:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[玉造温泉]] ゆ～ゆ ファイル:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[南紀白浜温泉]]「崎の湯」 ファイル:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[別府温泉]] [[竹瓦温泉]] ファイル:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|[[鉄輪温泉]] [[鉄輪むし湯]] </gallery>
== 関連項目 == * [[共同浴場]] - 温泉水を使用、地元民が管理する公衆浴場。 * [[日帰り入浴施設]] - 主に温泉水を使用し（使用していない場合もある）、企業等資本により建設された公衆浴場。 * [[秘湯]] * [[野湯]] * [[仙人温泉]]

revision.comment: 出典が全くないため資料を基に編集
revision.text:
[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[道後温泉]]本館（[[重要文化財]]）]] '''外湯'''（そとゆ）とは、[[温泉|温泉資源]]利用の一形態。温泉地内（湯元）に共同で使用される浴場（共同湯）を設ける形態をいう{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}。このような共同の浴場は歴史的には「外湯」のほか「元湯」や「総湯」とも称された。各宿泊施設内に設けられる[[内湯]]の[[対義語]]である。 == 概要 == 外湯と内湯の組み合わせは温泉地により異なり、温泉地の中心部に外湯を設ける例、温泉地の各所に湧出する泉源に外湯を設ける例、外湯と内湯が混在する例、内湯利用のみとする例の4つの型に分けられる。 機械による汲み上げが未だ無かった近世の温泉地では、湧出量が少なければ外湯として利用するほかなかった。近世半ば以降、社寺参詣や盛んになり諸街道が整備されたこともあって各地に[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]が設置されたが、旅籠内に必ずしも温泉が設けられるとは限らなかった。 近世には温泉療養（湯治客）が温泉利用の中心で、外湯は浴客の社交場でもあり温泉地のシンボルでもあった。しかし、[[明治時代]]以降、観光行楽目的の利用客が増えたことで内湯が日本全国に普及した。 近代以降になると機械による汲み上げが行われるようになり、温泉資源利用に大きな変化を生じた。 [[浅間温泉]]の場合、江戸時代から内湯と外湯（共同湯）があったが、明治初期には内湯が増加するとともに外湯も多く設置されるようになった{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2= 上野健一|title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}}。そこで湯株制が形成され、湯株仲間のみが利用できる外湯、入湯料を支払うことによって一般の利用者も利用できる外湯、地区住民の共有財産の外湯の3つのタイプが生じた。 温泉地によっては休息施設を併設した[[日帰り入浴施設]]も存在する。 == 外湯巡り == 以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。 * [[上山温泉]] * [[飯坂温泉]] * [[湯田中温泉]] * [[渋温泉]] * [[野沢温泉]] * [[越後湯沢温泉]] * [[伊東温泉]] * [[城崎温泉]] * [[南紀白浜温泉]] * [[別府温泉]]：[[竹瓦温泉]]、[[鉄輪むし湯]]などの市営温泉の他、別府市内には地元の人が管理する[[共同温泉]]が数百か所ある。 ** [[別府八湯温泉道]]：これらの共同温泉や旅館の立ち寄り湯など88の湯を巡り、温泉道名人の認定を目指すもの。 == ギャラリー == ファイル:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[有馬温泉]] [[金の湯]] ファイル:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|[[城崎温泉]]「さとの湯」 ファイル:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|[[野沢温泉]] 大湯 ファイル:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|[[三朝温泉]] たまわりの湯
（2023年3月閉館）{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} ファイル:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[玉造温泉]] ゆ～ゆ ファイル:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[南紀白浜温泉]]「崎の湯」 ファイル:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[別府温泉]] [[竹瓦温泉]] ファイル:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|[[鉄輪温泉]] [[鉄輪むし湯]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[共同浴場]] - 温泉水を使用、地元民が管理する公衆浴場。 * [[日帰り入浴施設]] - 主に温泉水を使用し（使用していない場合もある）、企業等資本により建設された公衆浴場。 * [[秘湯]] * [[野湯]] * [[仙人温泉]] {{温泉}} [[Category:温泉|そとゆ]]
{{Onsen}}

{{Onsen}}
[[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[ja:%E5%A4%96%E6%B9%AF]]

revision.text:
[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[道後温泉]]本館（[[重要文化財]]）]] '''外湯'''（そとゆ）とは、[[温泉|温泉資源]]利用の一形態。温泉地内（湯元）に共同で使用される浴場（共同湯）を設ける形態をいう{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}}。このような共同の浴場は歴史的には「外湯」のほか「元湯」や「総湯」とも称された。各宿泊施設内に設けられる[[内湯]]の[[対義語]]である。 == 概要 == 外湯と内湯の組み合わせは温泉地により異なり、温泉地の中心部に外湯を設ける例、温泉地の各所に湧出する泉源に外湯を設ける例、外湯と内湯が混在する例、内湯利用のみとする例の4つの型に分けられる。 機械による汲み上げが未だ無かった近世の温泉地では、湧出量が少なければ外湯として利用するほかなかった。近世半ば以降、社寺参詣や盛んになり諸街道が整備されたこともあって各地に[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]が設置されたが、旅籠内に必ずしも温泉が設けられるとは限らなかった。 近世には温泉療養（湯治客）が温泉利用の中心で、外湯は浴客の社交場でもあり温泉地のシンボルでもあった。しかし、[[明治時代]]以降、観光行楽目的の利用客が増えたことで内湯が日本全国に普及した。 近代以降になると機械による汲み上げが行われるようになり、温泉資源利用に大きな変化を生じた。 [[浅間温泉]]の場合、江戸時代から内湯と外湯（共同湯）があったが、明治初期には内湯が増加するとともに外湯も多く設置されるようになった{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2= 上野健一|title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}}。そこで湯株制が形成され、湯株仲間のみが利用できる外湯、入湯料を支払うことによって一般の利用者も利用できる外湯、地区住民の共有財産の外湯の3つのタイプが生じた。 温泉地によっては休息施設を併設した[[日帰り入浴施設]]も存在する。 == 外湯巡り == 以下の温泉地は、外湯巡りを温泉街の名物としている。 * [[上山温泉]] * [[飯坂温泉]] * [[湯田中温泉]] * [[渋温泉]] * [[野沢温泉]] * [[越後湯沢温泉]] * [[伊東温泉]] * [[城崎温泉]] * [[南紀白浜温泉]] * [[別府温泉]]：[[竹瓦温泉]]、[[鉄輪むし湯]]などの市営温泉の他、別府市内には地元の人が管理する[[共同温泉]]が数百か所ある。 ** [[別府八湯温泉道]]：これらの共同温泉や旅館の立ち寄り湯など88の湯を巡り、温泉道名人の認定を目指すもの。 == ギャラリー == ファイル:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[有馬温泉]] [[金の湯]] ファイル:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|[[城崎温泉]]「さとの湯」 ファイル:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|[[野沢温泉]] 大湯 ファイル:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|[[三朝温泉]] たまわりの湯
（2023年3月閉館）{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} ファイル:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[玉造温泉]] ゆ～ゆ ファイル:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[南紀白浜温泉]]「崎の湯」 ファイル:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[別府温泉]] [[竹瓦温泉]] ファイル:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|[[鉄輪温泉]] [[鉄輪むし湯]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[共同浴場]] - 温泉水を使用、地元民が管理する公衆浴場。 * [[日帰り入浴施設]] - 主に温泉水を使用し（使用していない場合もある）、企業等資本により建設された公衆浴場。 * [[秘湯]] * [[野湯]] * [[仙人温泉]] {{温泉}} [[Category:温泉|そとゆ]]

revision.text:
[[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|qq=|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|qq=|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|qq=|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|qq=|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|qq=|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|qq=|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|qq=|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] {{ill|Kin no Yu|ja|金の湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin no Yu|qq=}} File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|{{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu File:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|{{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} Tamawari no Yu
(Closed in March 2023){{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} File:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]] Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] “Saki no Yu” File:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|qq=}} File:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|{{ill|Kannawa Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Communal bathhouse|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal bathhouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} – Public baths that use hot spring water and are managed by local residents. * {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} – Public baths, often using hot springs (though sometimes not), built by private or corporate investment. * {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} * {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|qq=|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|qq=|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|qq=|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|qq=|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|qq=|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|qq=|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu onsen06s4592.jpg|[[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]] Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] “Saki no Yu” However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] {{ill|Kin no Yu|ja|金の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin no Yu|qq=}} File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|{{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu File:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|{{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} Tamawari no Yu
(Closed in March 2023){{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} File:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]] Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] “Saki no Yu” File:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|qq=}} File:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|{{ill|Kannawa Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Communal revision.text:
[[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] {{ill|Kin no Yu|ja|金の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin no Yu|qq=}} File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|{{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu File:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|{{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} Tamawari no Yu
(Closed in March 2023){{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} File:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]] Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] “Saki no Yu” Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Communal springs|qq=}} – Public baths that use hot spring water and are managed by local residents. * {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} – Public baths, often using hot springs (though sometimes not), built by private or corporate investment. * {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} * {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Communal springs|qq=}} – Public baths that use hot spring water and are managed by local residents. * {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try bathhouse|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal bathhouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:%E5%A4%96%E6%B9%AF]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] {{wikidata link|Q11430496}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata
