[[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] {{ill|Kin no Yu|ja|金の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin no Yu|qq=}} File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|{{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu File:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|{{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} Tamawari no Yu
(Closed in March 2023){{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} File:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]] Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] “Saki no Yu” File:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|qq=}} File:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|{{ill|Kannawa Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Communal [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:%E5%A4%96%E6%B9%AF]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11430496}}

[[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] revision.text:
[[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] {{ill|Kin no Yu|ja|金の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin no Yu|qq=}} File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|{{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu File:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|{{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} Tamawari no Yu
(Closed in March 2023){{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} File:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]] Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] “Saki no Yu” File:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|qq=}} File:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|{{ill|Kannawa Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Communal bathhouse|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal bathhouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} – Public baths that use hot spring water and are managed by local residents. * {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} – Public baths, often using hot springs (though sometimes not), built by private or corporate investment. * {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} * {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377677}} {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|外湯|version=100471545|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:%E5%A4%96%E6%B9%AF]] {{wikidata Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the revision.text:
[[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] {{ill|Kin no Yu|ja|金の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin no Yu|qq=}} File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|{{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu File:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|{{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} Tamawari no Yu
(Closed in March 2023){{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} File:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]] Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] “Saki no Yu” File:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|qq=}} File:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|{{ill|Kannawa Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Communal bathhouse|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal bathhouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} – Public baths that use hot spring water and are managed by local residents. * {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} – Public baths, often using hot springs (though sometimes not), built by private or corporate investment. * {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} * {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed revision.text:
[[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|[[:en:Dōgo Onsen|Dōgo Onsen]] Main Building ([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]])}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen resources]] usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At [[:en:Asama Onsen|Asama Onsen]], for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Iizaka Onsen|Iizaka Onsen]] * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] * [[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]]: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|[[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]] {{ill|Kin no Yu|ja|金の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin no Yu|qq=}} File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|{{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu File:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|{{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} Tamawari no Yu
(Closed in March 2023){{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} File:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|[[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]] Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|[[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|Nanki Shirahama Onsen]] “Saki no Yu” File:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|[[:en:Beppu Onsen|Beppu Onsen]] {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|qq=}} File:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|{{ill|Kannawa Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Communal bathhouse|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal bathhouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} – Public baths that use hot spring water and are managed by local residents. * {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} – Public baths, often using hot springs (though sometimes not), built by private or corporate investment. * {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} * {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
[[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=Dōgo Onsen}} Main Building ({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}})}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen resources}} usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At {{ill|Asama Onsen|en|Asama Onsen|WD=Q4803471|lt=Asama Onsen}}, for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Iizaka Onsen|en|Iizaka Onsen|WD=Q17192714|lt=Iizaka Onsen}} * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|en|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|WD=Q11408352|lt=Nanki Shirahama Onsen}} * {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}}: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|{{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|WD=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen}} {{ill|Kin no Yu|ja|金の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin no Yu|qq=}} File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|{{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu File:Misasa onsen13n4592.jpg|{{ill|Misasa Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasa Onsen|qq=}} Tamawari no Yu
(Closed in March 2023){{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} File:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|{{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|en|Tamatsukuri Onsen|WD=Q3514711|lt=Tamatsukuri Onsen}} Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|{{ill|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|en|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|WD=Q11408352|lt=Nanki Shirahama Onsen}} “Saki no Yu” File:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|{{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}} {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|qq=}} File:KannawaMushiyu.jpg|{{ill|Kannawa Onsen|ja|鉄輪温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa Onsen|qq=}} {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|qq=}} == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Communal bathhouse|ja|共同浴場|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|1=Communal hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal bathhouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal hot springs|qq=}} – Public baths that use hot spring water and are managed by local residents. * {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} – Public baths, often using hot springs (though sometimes not), built by private or corporate investment. * {{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}} * {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Sennin Onsen|ja|仙人温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennin Onsen|1=Sennin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377677}} {{Onsen}} [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430496]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|{{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=Dōgo Onsen}} Main Building ({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}})}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen resources}} usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At {{ill|Asama Onsen|en|Asama Onsen|WD=Q4803471|lt=Asama Onsen}}, for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Iizaka Onsen|en|Iizaka Onsen|WD=Q17192714|lt=Iizaka Onsen}} * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|en|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|WD=Q11408352|lt=Nanki Shirahama Onsen}} * {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}}: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|{{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|WD=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen}} {{ill|Kin no Yu|ja|金の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin no Yu|qq=}} File:Kinosaki satonoyu01 2048.jpg|{{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} “Satonoyu” File:Nozawaonsen ohyu.jpg|{{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} Ōyu File:Misasa (Closed in March 2023){{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nnn.co.jp/articles/-/318771 |title=たまわりの湯跡地 売却へ 神戸の企業に 地元おおむね了承 三朝町|accessdate=2024-05-23 |publisher=日本海新聞 |language=ja}} File:Tamatsukuri onsen06s4592.jpg|{{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|en|Tamatsukuri Onsen|WD=Q3514711|lt=Tamatsukuri Onsen}} Yū~Yū File:131221 Shirahama Onsen Shirahama Wakayama pref Japan17bs.jpg|{{ill|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|en|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|WD=Q11408352|lt=Nanki Shirahama Onsen}} “Saki no Yu” File:Beppu Takegawara-onsen02s5s4272.jpg|{{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}} Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. In early modern hot spring areas (when water pumps did not yet exist), a low outflow volume of hot spring water made it inevitable to rely on communal use as Sotoyu. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At {{ill|Asama Onsen|en|Asama Onsen|WD=Q4803471|lt=Asama Onsen}}, for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Iizaka Onsen|en|Iizaka Onsen|WD=Q17192714|lt=Iizaka Onsen}} * {{ill|Yudanaka Onsen|ja|湯田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudanaka Onsen|1=Yudanaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563754}} * {{ill|Shibu Onsen|ja|渋温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibu Onsen|1=Shibu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561642}} * {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|ja|越後湯沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|1=Echigo Yuzawa Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636518}} * {{ill|Itō Onsen|ja|伊東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Onsen|1=Itō Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11266957}} * {{ill|Kinosaki Onsen|ja|城崎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinosaki Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|en|Nanki-Shirahama Onsen|WD=Q11408352|lt=Nanki Shirahama Onsen}} * {{ill|Beppu Onsen|en|Beppu Onsen|WD=Q4890932|lt=Beppu Onsen}}: In addition to the city-operated {{ill|Takegawara Onsen|ja|竹瓦温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takegawara Onsen|1=Takegawara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11599290}} and {{ill|Kannawa mushiyu|ja|鉄輪むし湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannawa mushiyu|1=Kannawa Steam Bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649634}}, there are hundreds of {{ill|Communal onsen|ja|共同温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Communal onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同浴場|1=communal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392653}} in Beppu City run by local residents. ** {{ill|Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|ja|別府八湯温泉道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Hatto Onsen Dō|1=Beppu Hatto Onsen Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396699}}: Visitors can try 88 different baths, including these communal ones and day-use baths at ryokan, aiming for recognition as a “Hot Spring Master.” == Gallery == File:Kinnoyu01s1920.jpg|{{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] [[ja:外湯]] refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Outdoor hot spring baths|ja|露天風呂|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|1=Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|13=User:Immanuelle/Outdoor hot spring baths|qq=}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}[[File:Dōgo Onsen.jpg|250px|thumb|{{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=Dōgo Onsen|1=Dōgo Onsen}} Main Building ({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}})}]] '''Sotoyu''' (外湯) is one form of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen resources|1=Onsen}} usage. It refers to a style in which a communal bathing facility (communal bath) is established collectively for use in a hot spring area (the source of the spring, or yumoto). Historically, such communal baths have also been called “motoyu” or “sōyu,” in addition to “Sotoyu.”{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://doi.org/10.6012/jwei.31.84|author=高柳友彦 |title=温泉資源管理の歴史 |journal=水資源・環境研究 |volume=31 |issue=2 |publisher=水資源・環境学会 |date=2018 |pages=84-87 |naid= |ref=}}{{Cite journal |和書 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol32_pdf/vol32no2_052_060.pdf|author=益子安 |title=温泉の集中管理 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=32 |issue=2 |publisher=日本温泉科学会 |date=1981 |pages=52-60 |naid= |ref=}} It is the counterpart to the {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} that is typically set inside lodging facilities. == Overview == In combining Sotoyu and internal baths, there are four main patterns depending on the hot spring area: (1) establishing a central communal bath in the heart of the hot spring town; (2) setting up multiple baths at various hot spring sources located across the area; (3) a mix of communal and in-house facilities; or (4) places using only in-house baths. From around the mid-early modern era onward, as shrine and temple pilgrimages became popular and roads were better maintained, {{ill|Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|ja|湯治#湯治場|lt=Tōji facility|lt_ja=湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji (hot spring therapy)#Tōji facility|1=Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} sprang up in many regions. However, not all lodging facilities at that time necessarily installed their own hot spring baths. During the early modern period, therapeutic bathing (tōji guests) was the main use of hot springs, making communal baths a social gathering spot for bathers and a symbol of the hot spring area. After the start of the Meiji era, however, with an increase in travelers for tourism and leisure, the use of in-house baths (internal baths) spread nationwide in Japan. In modern times and beyond, mechanical pumping of hot spring water began, resulting in major changes to how hot spring resources were utilized. At {{ill|Asama Onsen|en|Asama Onsen|WD=Q4803471|lt=Asama Onsen|1=Asama Onsen}}, for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the At {{ill|Asama Onsen|en|Asama Onsen|WD=Q4803471|lt=Asama Onsen|1=Asama Onsen}}, for instance, both internal baths and communal baths (Sotoyu) existed as far back as the Edo period, and in the early Meiji period, the number of internal baths rose while communal baths also became more numerous.{{Cite journal |和書 |url=https://www.geoenv.tsukuba.ac.jp/~chicho/nenpo/7/08.pdf|author1=井田仁康 |author2=上野健一 |title=浅間温泉の形成過程と集落構造 |journal=地域調査報告 |volume= |issue= |publisher=筑波大学 |date=1985|pages=|naid= |ref=}} This led to the development of a system of “bath share certificates,” giving rise to three types of external communal baths: those used only by certificate holders, those open to general users upon payment of a bathing fee, and those managed as jointly owned assets of local residents. Some hot spring areas also feature {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} with rest spaces attached. == Sotoyu Tour (外湯巡り) == The following hot spring areas highlight visits to their communal baths as a hallmark of the town: * {{ill|Kaminoyama Onsen|ja|上山温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminoyama Onsen|qq=}} * {{ill|Iizaka Onsen|en|Iizaka Onsen|WD=Q17192714|lt=Iizaka Onsen|1=Iizaka Onsen}} * {{ill|Yudanaka
